coredata.dc:title: Investigation on thermal performance analysis of a pouch Li-ion battery under various drive cycle profiles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahjalal, Mohammad
Tripathy, Yashraj
Sheikh, Muhammad
coredata.dc:description: Knowledge of accurate battery temperature is essential for enabling safe operation of batteries in electrified vehicles application under aggressive drive profiles. Thermal modelling of batteries based on mere joule heating term is usually unable to compensate for resulting inaccuracy due to neglecting entropic heat in batteries. Inclusion of entropic effect into the thermal model derived from simple experiments can help accurate estimate of battery temperature in early design stage. This paper presents a lumped thermal model incorporating entropic effect to predict transient thermal behaviors of pouch cell under various aggressive loads. Compared with experimental results simulation model can predict results with acceptable accuracy and minimal computing cost. The simulation results with these tested conditions are found in good agreement with experimental results with a maximum RMSE error of 0.15, 0.07, 0.29, and 0.26 respectively. This shows the inclusion of reversible heat in the enhancing thermal prediction performance of the proposed electro-thermal model is significant and is a fairly good candidate for a rapid electro-thermal simulation of Li-ion pouch type batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery modelling
Li-ion battery
Thermal modelling
Experimental measurement
Temperature
Entropic effects

Equivalent electric thermal model of the battery.

Transient cooling curve of Li-NMC pouch cell.
Integrated electro-thermal model.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RMSE error profile of predicted voltage and temperature under different drive cycles.

head.ce:title: Investigation on thermal performance analysis of a pouch Li-ion battery under various drive cycle profiles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: WMG, University of Warwick, Coventry CV4 7AL, United Kingdom
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: WMG, University of Warwick, Coventry CV4 7AL, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Knowledge of accurate battery temperature is essential for enabling safe operation of batteries in electrified vehicles application under aggressive drive profiles. Thermal modelling of batteries based on mere joule heating term is usually unable to compensate for resulting inaccuracy due to neglecting entropic heat in batteries. Inclusion of entropic effect into the thermal model derived from simple experiments can help accurate estimate of battery temperature in early design stage. This paper presents a lumped thermal model incorporating entropic effect to predict transient thermal behaviors of pouch cell under various aggressive loads. Compared with experimental results simulation model can predict results with acceptable accuracy and minimal computing cost. The simulation results with these tested conditions are found in good agreement with experimental results with a maximum RMSE error of 0.15, 0.07, 0.29, and 0.26 respectively. This shows the inclusion of reversible heat in the enhancing thermal prediction performance of the proposed electro-thermal model is significant and is a fairly good candidate for a rapid electro-thermal simulation of Li-ion pouch type batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The present economic growth is solely based on fossil fuel based and more economic development activities demand for further exploitation of fossil fuel. This has made energy sector less stable due to depletion of fossil fuel as well as lack of intermittent supply of fossil fuel from geopolitically unstable regions. Zero-emission vehicle has been global consensus among the government and policy makers to tackle growing climate change and CO2 emissions. This context sets transport sector to be transformed into fully electric. The fully electrified vehicle can bring more economic stability and environmental benefits. For example, the fossil fuel based transportation can be translated either hybrid or fully electric vehicle and thus taking the peer pressure on diesel or petroleum considerably can leverage lesser fuel consumption [1]. A key element of an electrified vehicle is lithium-ion batteries which provide higher energy and power density, higher efficiency and lower self-discharge compared to others battery technologies (NiCd, NiMH, and Lead Acid) [2–5]. However, performance of Li-ion battery is thermally constraint. For example, Li-ion batteries often experience cold and hot climate which can result in capacity loss and accelerated degradation possibly leading to thermal runaway and explosion so on [6,7]. If temperature issues left untreated, for Li-ion batteries, the cell temperature not only impacts the electrical parameters of the batteries in operation such as open circuit voltage, internal resistance, and available capacity, but also could lead to stringent battery reliability concerns [8,9]. Therefore, formulating and developing an accurate electro-thermal model is needed at early-stage design to extend the battery life and to ensure the battery temperature within safe thermal window during all operating conditions such as cruise, acceleration, braking, etc.

From thermal perspective, heat generation needs to be estimated accurately. The consequence of inaccurate heat estimation can be severely thermally problematic because it dictates the thermal model performance. In low charge/discharge cases, reversible heat due to entropic change is dominant. On the other hand, inaccurate thermal prediction can introduce cascaded design error in battery health and lifetime monitoring schemes.

Existing electro-thermal models found in the literature can be categorized into two groups which are high-fidelity electrochemical model and circuit network based electrical model. Electrochemical/physics-based model can be represented by incorporating differential equations and thermal effects can be taken into account [10–12]. These are relatively accurate but requires high computation load as the models generate detailed mesh containing cell area, electrode porosity, electrolyte thickness, material density, heat capacity and thermal conductivity etc. The simplified thermal model can be derived by conducting thermal experiments on a dedicated test bench. But this involves costly thermal chamber and other accessories for temperature monitoring by embedded thermocouples on battery surface [13,14].

Electric based model can be introduced as electric-circuit equivalent models [15,16]. These models are easy to implement. However, to include thermal effects, model parameter needs to be represented as temperature dependent. This involves expensive experimentation method such as thermal chamber and calorimeter dedicated to parameter identification that can produce results as close to physics-based models [17].

Several research attempts focused on developing observer-based estimation techniques to find an alternative of the thermal chambers. These approaches are dedicated to deriving required parameter to construct electrical battery-based model. For example, online estimation of battery parameters from several charge/discharge tests can be possible by using combined approach of an adaptive least-square algorithm and state observers [14]. An improved version of state observer technique such as sliding mode observer is outlined in [18]. One of the key issues with these approaches is the short sampling time is needed to precisely estimate the transient model parameters. This can offset the accuracy of the battery dynamic model.

Machine learning technique can be the alternative approach of accurate parameter identification. These include data-based neural network, sparse Bayesian predictive model, and rule-based fuzzy logic [19,20]. However, these approaches are complex in terms of implementation and requires large number of training datasets for parameter identification.

Many thermal models exist where simplified electrochemical equations are coupled with a view to obtaining the battery temperature profile under various load conditions. For example, presented thermal model found in the literature includes a prismatic LFP battery [21], commercial 18,650 LFP cell [22], and one special cases for LFP pouch cells by lumped capacitance method [23]. Regarding NMC chemistry, 2D-NMC thermal model is proposed in [23]. One dimensional thermal model is also presented in the literature [23,24]. An improved thermal model related to pouch cell is proposed in [25] and another work related to cylindrical cell is described in [26]. However, despite the efficiency of the above-mentioned thermal models, their high computational cost arising from finite volume method comes with the drawback that such a model cannot be readily implemented to different standard drive cycles which is normally long-term profile and thus, loses the capacity for further use in real-time estimation. Also, the entropic effect on slow charging/discharging is not included or evaluated. The entropic effect is not even fully documented to pouch cell under various drive cycles. Furthermore, heat generation and its impact on the voltage behavior is not well-studied yet. Given these observations, this paper focuses on inclusion of entropic effect and solving with integrated electro-thermal model which will predict battery temperature under various operating conditions with a minimal computing cost at MATLAB/Simulink environment.

From the literature mentioned above, one thing is obvious that most electrical models are based on mainly joule heating terms. Thus, total heat estimation is not accurate particularly for slow charging of EV batteries usually at office or home. To ensure BMS functionality to protect battery cells damaged caused from overheating accurate thermal model is significant. To get accurate thermal model, quantification of heat source is equally important. The heat source is not only generated due to joule heating but also due to entropy change. When battery is slowly charged the temperature of the battery can decrease at high state of charge and increase at low state of charge due to the strong effect of entropy change [27–29]. Therefore, the entropic heat modelling is significant to quantify accurately heat source term and thus achieve a better thermal model accuracy. Besides the reaping benefits of accurate thermal model, entropic profile of battery can be used to study the battery ageing and degradation mechanism [30] since it can reveal crystal structural changes in the electrodes [31–35].

In this paper, a generic electro-thermal model is proposed which has modelled entropic heat and included in the heat generation term to increase temperature prediction accuracy. The advantage with the proposed model is the fact that the model is able to represent the impact of entropic heat on the battery performance using a simplified lumped thermal model constructed based on running few simple experiments.

The key contribution of this paper is modelling of entropic heat and development of the integrated electro-thermal model, based on battery datasheet and simple experiments and the fast thermal analysis under different drive cycle profiles (computation time is less than 1 s in standard desktop computer arising from the RC thermal network). The proposed approach is validated experimentally and can be applied to any format of Li-ion pouch cell battery types and easily implementable in real time drive cycle profiles due to providing fast estimation of temperature prediction with acceptable accuracy which is key requirement in BMS.

This paper is organized as follows. Section II describes the cell level electro-thermal model, which includes the cell electrical parameter, entropic effect, heat generation, and thermal models. The battery cell thermal model is then constructed in Section III, and Section IV focuses on the integrated electro-thermal model, from which the cell temperature is estimated. Each model is identified and validated in its corresponding section.
Understanding the electrical and thermal behavior of the Li-ion pouch cell is critical in the design of a robust battery pack that meets the load requirements.

In this paper, an electro-thermal model methodology has been developed and applied to predict cell surface temperature of a pouch cell. A full outline of modelling methodology is illustrated in Fig. 1 . This approach requires the cell electrical parameter and thermal parameters. To construct electrical model, experiment tests have been performed to obtain electrical parameters that describe the battery behavior. Then thermal models have been constructed from experiments. The reversible heat loss model has been included in the model by determining entropic coefficient from the potentiometric method. Then, performance of the proposed electro-thermal model under various load profiles have been analyzed in the experiments.

The proposed electro-thermal model is implemented in Matlab/Simulink platform. For this, two models (electrical and thermal) are coupled together and solved and later model accuracy under different load case scenario is validated through the experiments.

The total heat generation of a pouch cell battery can be quantified by two parts, first part represents the irreversible heat
Q irr
dictated by over potential and the reversible heat
Q rev
caused by entropy change, and their contributions can be summed up and quantified by Eq. (1)
(1)
Q =
Q irr
+
Q rev
=
I cell
V −
U OCV
+
I cell
T cell
d
U OCV
dT
where
I cell
is the current passing through the battery,
T cell
is the cell surface temperature of the battery, V is the terminal voltage and
U OCV
is the open circuit potential of the battery cell in equilibrium state. In this work, the current
I cell
is considered as positive when the battery is being charged and
Q rev
is positive when heat is dissipated from the battery system to the environment, as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

In Eq. (1)
d
U OCV
dT
is the entropic coefficient which can be defined as entropy change of the reaction by Eq. (2)
(2)
d
U OCV
dT
=
∆ S
nF
where
d
U OCV
dT
is the entropy change in the battery cell, n is the number of electrons transferred per atom and F is the Farday constant. Depending on the sign of the current
I cell
and the entropic coefficient
d
U OCV
dT
, the entropy heat can be either positive or negative. As a consequence, the overpotential of the battery contributes to the irreversible heat generation, i.e., joule heating losses from the battery. It is mainly ohmic drop that happens in the current collectors, contact resistance between the active particles, kinetics of charge transfer on the phase boundary and finally the mass transport of lithium ions in both electrolyte and solid particles. The over potential behaves non-linearly with the cell current, sign of the current, state of charge (SOC) and temperature etc. On the contrary, the reversible heat shows linear relationship and the entropic coefficient
d
U OCV
dT
varies linearly with SOC only. When the cell current is very low, irreversible heat is not significant and the reversible heat is dominant in the heat generation. With the increasing current, irreversible heat is dominant, and the entropic heating is less significant.

To address entropy change first six cells were first discharged to 0 % SOC at C/10 rate and charged to 100 % SOC at C/10 rate. Fig. 3 describes the obtained temperature for C/10 discharge and C/10 charge. It should be noted that temperature rise is noticeable at the lower SOC range. At the initial charge stage, at lower SOC range, temperature dip is noticed. This is obviously due to entropy change. In discharge state, at the lower SOC end the discharging current considered as negative and negative entropy sign contributes the reversible loss positively in total heat generation and thus results in battery heating and as consequence temperature rise is noticed. Temperature dip can be explained combination of the charging current taken into account positively and negative entropy sign contributes the reversible loss negatively in total heat generation and thus results in battery cooling.

In order to estimate entropy profiles, potentiometric method is used. In this method open circuit potential after each temperature step is recorded and the slope of the linear relationship between open circuit potential and battery temperature is defined as the entropic coefficient. The experiment equipment used for conducting potentiometric test is battery cycler with a programmable climate chamber ESPEC. The voltage measurement accuracy limit is within ± 1 mv . Temperature is measured by T-type thermocouple with accuracy of ± 1 ° C . In the experiment, six pouch cells are tested and are oriented in test bed horizontally. The entropic coefficient is estimated for 11 SOC levels from 100 % to 0 % SOC level. The complete experimental setup for this method is shown in Fig. 4 .

The benchmark method for identification of entropy coefficient has been described in Fig. 5 . In applying this method, the main steps are given below:
1.
Discharge all 6 cells to SOC 0 % at C/10 rate
2.
Charge all 6 cells to SOC 100 % at C/10 rate
3.
Charge to SOC 100 % by CC-CV charging method (using constant current constant voltage charging method)
4.
Leave the cell at 25 °C for 1 day
5.
Soak at 25 °C, 15 °C, 5 °C and −5 °C for 8 h and recording cell voltage, cell temperature, and ambient temperature, respectively.
6.
Leave all the 6 cells at 25 °C for rest period of 8 h
7.
Discharge all the 6 cells 10 % SOC decrement from previous SOC for 6 min
8.
Repeat the test steps 5 and 6 until SOC reaching up to 0 %

To describe this method for a SOC range starting from 50 % to 30 % battery SOC, the above mentioned steps are applied. At each SOC level, the open circuit potential is recorded at 1 s sampling rate with respect to applied thermal cycling. Then, from the experimental data sets the open circuit potential is pulled for the corresponding thermal cycling temperature data and then the slope of this linear relationship between open circuit potential and temperature is defined as entropic coefficient. Raw open circuit voltage and input temperature vs. time during thermal cycling for 50–40–30 % SOC values are shown in Fig. 6 and fitted to the Eq. (3). Entropic estimation from the gradient is illustrated in Fig. 7 .
(3)
y = mx + c
where m = gradient = entropic coefficient =
d
U OCV
dT
.

The results from the potentiometric method for six cells are shown in Fig. 8 and there is no significant difference in estimated entropy profiles between the six battery cells. The entropic coefficient of the measured battery cell is within ± 0.4
mv
K
− 1
so the voltage change caused by the temperature is much smaller compared to the pouch cell battery working voltage range (2.7 V–4.2 V).
In this work, the 60 Ah pouch cell battery is investigated, and the chemistry type is Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC). The electrical and mechanical parameter of the cell are listed in Table 1 .

The second order equivalent circuit model (ECM) battery model is employed in this work. The electrical model is illustrated in Fig. 9 which consists of an open-circuit voltage, two resistor-capacitor networks and an ohmic resistor. First RC network branch describes charge transfer and double layer effect which can be considered as fast dynamics. Second RC network branch describes battery diffusion effect which can be treated as slow dynamics. The battery OCV also depends on the SOC, i.e., OCV = f(SOC).

Since drive cycles are applied at 25 ° C , electrical characterization is performed at constant 25 ° C . The resistance is expressed as function of state of charge only R = f(SOC) Through proper experimental design, ECM parameters have been identified. In this work, the 10s discharge pulse test is adopted to extract ECM parameter over the whole SOC range of the battery. Initially the battery is fully charged from 0 % SOC to 100 % SOC via constant current-constant voltage charging method. The test procedure involves SOC adjustment, rest phase, discharge pulse phase and relaxation phase. To set the battery at defined 10 % SOC decrement cells are discharged for 6 min and 30 min of rest period is added. Then, the cells are ready for characterization at predefined SOC. Here, one discharge pulse period of 10s at 1C rate is applied and followed by rest period for 30 min. For discharging the battery from 100 % to 0 % SOC, 11 discharge pulses were applied for SOC adjustment. A single discharge pulse event for battery parameter test procedure at predefined is illustrated Fig. 10 . For example, cells at 100 % SOC are reached at 90 % SOC by applying a discharge pulse for 6 min (SOC adjustment @ 1C rate) and followed by a rest period of 30 min. After reaching 90 % SOC, cells are characterized by applying the 10s discharge pulse period @1C rate and following the relaxation phase of 30 min. During the applied discharge pulse, the cell voltage falls abruptly from
V 1
to
V 2
. This abrupt voltage drop can be explained by the battery's internal ohmic resistance (
R 0
) and can be estimated as
V 1
−
V 2
/
I
dch _ pulse
. As the battery discharges further, and the battery terminal voltage reaches at
V 3
. This gradual voltage drop can be explained by battery polarization resistance (
R p
) in electrodes and can be estimated as
V 2
−
V 3
/
I
dch _ pulse
. Since first order ECM model is not sufficient for capturing the dynamic responses of battery, second order ECM model is considered in this work.

The terminal voltage during the relaxation period can be fitted with the exponential function detailed in Eq. (4).
(4)
V batt
= a − b . exp
− c . t
− d . exp
− e . t

The terminal voltage at relaxation period can be rewritten as Eq. (5).
(5)
V batt
=
V OCV

R 0
.

dch − pulse

dch − pulse

From the equation stated above the ECM parameter values for
R 0

R 2
,
C 1
and

are obtained from Eq. (6).
(6)
a =
V OCV

R 2

A curve fitting code programmed by MATLAB environment is used to fit the voltage response. In this work, nonlinear least square method is chosen, and the voltage response is fitted to the Eq. (4). The ECM Model parameter values
R 0
,
R 1
,
R 2
,
C 1
and
C 2
are obtained from the coefficients a , b , c , d , and
e . Finally, the extracted parameters are used in the battery model simulation.

The identified parameters are illustrated in Fig. 11 . As can be seen from the figure, the battery resistance (R o) shows consistent value over the entire SOC range from 5 to 95 %. On the other hand, polarization resistance (R 1) shows decreasing trend from 20 % to 40 %, then increasing trend from 40 % 60 % and again decreasing trend from 60 % to 100 %. The polarization resistance does not change significantly. The reason is to be believed that the battery tests are performed here through the discharge pulse-rest test at constant temperature (25 °C). We have chosen this temperature 25 °C because our applied drive cycle profiles are tested also at 25 °C. Ideally, all the battery parameters described above should be multivariable functions of SOC, Temperature (T) and the C-rate of the load current. However, inclusion of all these factors not only makes the battery parameter estimation process complicated but also increases both the time and computational load of the BMS. Hence, within certain error bound, some relationships can be simplified.

The diffusion resistance (R 2) shows decreasing trend from 5 to 40 % and the increasing trend from 40 % to 60 % and again decreasing trend from 60 % to 100 %. The polarization time constant shows constant values over the entire SOC range from 5 to 95 %. While diffusion time constant shows varying values, decreasing trend starts from 20 till about 40 % SOC and the increasing trend from 40 % SOC till 60 % SOC. Over 60 % SOC another decreasing trend is observed.

In the electrical model of a battery, the battery voltage is a result of battery constant voltage, charge depletion and the exponential charge extraction zone. Since OCV is an important characteristic parameter of the battery, it is important to identify the relationship between OCV and SOC. Fig. 12 illustrates, battery open circuit voltage (OCV) vs. SOC characteristics.

To estimate cell surface temperature by MATLAB/Simulink model, one order RC lumped thermal model has been used and thermal model parameters have been derived from the test procedure adopted from [5]. The thermal equivalent electric circuits for a single cell in a battery pack is shown in Fig. 13 .

To precisely estimate battery temperature a thermal model is required. To determine the thermal model parameters (
R th
and
t c
), an easiest and simple thermal test was conducted at ambient climatic temperature. The idea is to raise the battery temperature 10 ° C above ambient temperature. To achieve this test performs discharging/charging the Li-ion battery at the same rate. Later, the test was stopped during discharge/charge when the battery temperature reaches again close to the ambient climatic temperature. Fig. 13 depicts expected waveforms of temperatures (ambient air and battery), voltage, and current.

From Fig. 14 (a), when the discharge is stopped at time
t 1
, the cooling time constant after a time interval
t c
can be derived using Eq. (16). From the cooling time constant using Eq. (17), if
R th
can be estimated then it is easily
C th
identifiable. Fig. 15 describes the transient cooling curve for the pouch cell. Table 2 summarizes the thermal properties of pouch cell.

The generated heat in the battery can be categorized to reversible (
Q rev
W
) and irreversible heat (
Q irrev
W
) which a part of it is dissipated either by natural convection or active cooling. Table 1 lists the thermal model parameters have been used in this work.

C p

The integrated electro-thermal model is shown in Fig. 16 . The battery electrical model is built based on experimental data. This model is parameterized in a way to better represent the effect of SOC (state of charge) and DOD (depth of discharge) on the battery open circuit voltage. Electrical model provides two voltage output such as open circuit potential and battery terminal voltage. Difference of these two multiplied by current profile provides the irreversible hat generation term and to make heat generation model accurate, entropic heat is included. To take account of reversible losses, the look-up table based entopic table in terms of SOC is added into the model. The thermal model providing cell surface temperature is feedback into the electrical model while computing entropic heat.

The proposed electro-thermal model developed for pouch cells are validated by comparing the model temperatures with the measured temperatures for various drive profile conditions. These include Artemis 130 kph, Artemis Extra urban, Cruise 160 kph, Leamington Warwick, WLTC etc. The test set up schematic for drive profile implementation to the battery under test is shown in Fig. 4. The main steps are as follows:
➢
The environmental test chamber is used to vary the battery ambient air temperature.
➢
The programmable power supply from Bitrode battery cycler is used to charge the battery
➢
The drive cycle profile is converted and represented as the current profile with respect to time and applied to the battery through channels powered by the cycler central program.
➢
All battery data (voltage, current, SOC, temperature, etc.) can be accessed from the cycler through data obtained through the cycler CAN-bus.
➢
The cell surface temperatures are measured by the embedded thermocouples and all temperature sensors data are recorded through picologger set up attached to laptop via USB interface

The current and voltage profiles for these drive cycles are shown in Figs. 17–21
. It is worth mentioning that there are slight differences between the simulated and measured temperatures. In particular, the highest root-mean-square errors (RMSE) of simulated and measured surface temperatures are 0.6383, 0.6383 respectively. These differences may be explained with the inclusion of estimated constant convective heat transfer coefficient. In practice, this is time varying. Nevertheless, since the comparison of temperatures shows a good agreement and reasonably small RMSEs, it can be concluded that the proposed electro-thermal model with identified lumped thermal approach is reasonably accurate for thermal management during various drive cycles.

The electro-thermal model is also tested with a high/low discharge/charge profile. In this case, C/2 has been considered as high discharge/charge profile and C/10 has been considered as low discharge/charge profile. Figs. 22–25
show the simulation and experimental results at 23 ° C ambient air temperatures. The RMSE error profiles of predicted voltage and temperature for all cases are listed in Table 3 .

This paper presented a generic electro-thermal battery model for fast and accurate prediction of cell temperature. Compared to state-of-the-art electro-thermal Li-ion battery models, the proposed model includes entropic heating which corrects the thermal model inaccuracy due to only including irreversible heat. The main contribution of the paper is accurate estimation of temperature profiles under aggressive drive profiles. Additionally thermal model is simply constructed performing simple experiments. The proposed model was validated experimentally using a 4.2 V 54 Ah, Li-NMC pouch cells. The simulation results for different drive profiles compared to experimental results are within ±1 %. To gain more confidence in the model, test was conducted under low/high charge/discharge profiles. The simulation results with these tested conditions are found in good agreement with experimental results with a maximum RMSE error of 0.15, 0.07, 0.29, and 0.26 respectively. This shows the inclusion of reversible heat in the integrated electro-thermal model is significant for predicting accurate temperature profile and is found to be a fairly good candidate for a rapid electro-thermal simulation of Li-ion batteries under aggressive drive profiles, where irreversible heat of the battery is insufficient to predict accurate temperature profile.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
